title: Proactive Outpatient Advance Care Planning at a Medicare Annual Wellness Visit for a CAPABLE 75-Year-Old Man with Chronic Heart Failure, CKD Stage 3, and Early Mild Cognitive Impairment (No Advance Directive on File): Distinguishing the LIVING WILL from the DURABLE POWER OF ATTORNEY FOR HEALTHCARE, Adding a POLST/MOLST as a Portable Clinician's Order, Confirming Decision-Making Capacity, Eliciting Values and Goals of Care, Selecting and EDUCATING a Surrogate, Clarifying What a DNR/DNAR Order Does and Does NOT Mean, and Ensuring Cross-Setting Portability — All Without Coercion
subtitle: ADVANCE CARE PLANNING (ACP) is the process by which a person clarifies and communicates their values, goals, and treatment preferences for FUTURE medical care in case they lose decision-making capacity — and identifies a SURROGATE who can speak for them. Done PROACTIVELY in the outpatient setting with a CAPABLE patient (and not waiting for a crisis or surrogate conflict), ACP preserves autonomy, reduces decisional burden on family, aligns care with values, and decreases unwanted aggressive treatment at the end of life. This case is the proactive outpatient/wellness-visit ACP conversation — distinct from acute family conflict over honoring an existing directive (case-092), surrogate decision-making when the patient already LACKS capacity (case-132), and capacity assessment for a current decision/'dignity of risk' (case-116). TWO BROAD CATEGORIES of document apply. (A) ADVANCE DIRECTIVES — completed by any adult with capacity, for FUTURE possible incapacity: a LIVING WILL (instructional directive) states what treatments the patient WOULD WANT or REFUSE under specific future conditions (e.g., mechanical ventilation, artificial nutrition/hydration, dialysis, antibiotics in terminal illness); a DURABLE POWER OF ATTORNEY FOR HEALTHCARE (DPOA-HC / healthcare proxy / agent) names a SURROGATE authorized to make decisions if the patient cannot — high yield: a DPOA-HC alone is often more useful than a living will because no document can anticipate every clinical scenario, and a well-informed surrogate can. (B) POLST / MOLST (Physician/Medical Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment) is NOT an advance directive — it is a portable, signed CLINICIAN'S ORDER for CURRENT treatment preferences in seriously ill or frail patients; it covers CPR (Attempt vs Do Not Attempt), level of intervention (Full / Selective / Comfort-focused), and medically administered nutrition; it TRAVELS across settings (home, EMS, ED, hospital, SNF), is followed by emergency responders, and SUPPLEMENTS (does NOT replace) an advance directive. Documents are STATE-SPECIFIC (witnesses, notary, forms): execute per state law and review when moving states. CONFIRM CAPACITY first (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate a stable choice) — capacity is DECISION-SPECIFIC and presumed; mild cognitive impairment ALONE, an unwise choice, age, or disagreement do NOT equal incapacity (link to [[case-116]]). ELICIT VALUES and goals (open questions: 'what makes life meaningful?' 'what worries you most?' 'what trade-offs would you accept?'), explore cultural, religious, and personal context, and use plain language at the patient's health-literacy level. EDUCATE about code status: a DNR/DNAR/AND order limits ONLY cardiopulmonary resuscitation in cardiac arrest — it does NOT mean 'no care' (other treatments continue per the plan), does NOT require unit transfer, and does NOT preclude hospice/aggressive comfort care. SURROGATE SELECTION + EDUCATION: choose someone who knows the patient's values, is willing and able to advocate, can manage stress, and is reachable; the patient should EDUCATE the surrogate about his values so the surrogate can apply SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT later. Document and ensure CROSS-SETTING PORTABILITY: file in the chart/EHR, give copies to the patient/family/healthcare agent, send copies to PCP and specialists and the local hospital, and consider a wallet card; a properly signed POLST belongs visibly in the home for EMS. Frame ACP as an ongoing PROCESS, not a one-time form: revisit at the next Annual Wellness Visit and around the 'five Ds' — Decade (new), Death (in family), Divorce, Diagnosis (new), Decline — and when goals change. Respect AUTONOMY including the right to DEFER or DECLINE ACP — never coerce; never sign documents the patient does not understand. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: making decisions for him 'because he's old and sick'; treating mild cognitive impairment as automatic incapacity and bypassing him; pressuring a specific code status; telling him a DNR means no care/transfer; completing documents but never sharing them across settings (the most common operational failure); confusing POLST with an advance directive or expecting a living will to anticipate every scenario. NURSING ROLE: confirm capacity, elicit values, educate the patient/family in plain language about advance directives + POLST/MOLST + code status, support surrogate selection and surrogate education, coordinate proper document execution per state law, ensure cross-setting portability, document objectively, and treat ACP as an ongoing values-based conversation.

patient.name: K. Pelletier
patient.code: Outpatient (Medicare Annual Wellness Visit); full code currently; goal = proactive advance care planning with a CAPABLE older adult to clarify values, complete an advance directive (living will + durable POA-HC), sign a POLST as appropriate, select and educate a surrogate, ensure cross-setting portability, and respect autonomy without coercion
patient.admitDate: Seen for his Medicare Annual Wellness Visit. PMH: chronic heart failure NYHA Class II (LVEF 38%), CKD stage 3a (baseline eGFR ~48), HTN, mild cognitive impairment (formal evaluation confirms decision-making CAPACITY preserved). NO advance directive, no healthcare proxy, and no POLST on file. He arrives with his wife of 50 years; an adult daughter (out of state) joins by phone. He is alert, engaged, and asks 'I want to get my paperwork in order in case something happens.' The clinic RN facilitates a comprehensive advance care planning conversation (capacity confirmation, values clarification, document education, surrogate selection and education, POLST consideration, document execution, and portability planning) without coercion, while respecting his autonomy.
patient.room: Outpatient primary-care clinic; private exam room; interdisciplinary support available (PCP, clinic RN, social work, chaplain on request); patient and wife present, adult daughter on speakerphone

36.8°C; documents executed per state-specific law (witnesses/notary as required); POLST signed by the PCP for SELECTIVE INTERVENTION + Do Not Attempt CPR consistent with his stated wishes; copies filed in chart/EHR + given to patient/wife/daughter + shared with cardiology + local hospital; portable card carried; POLST visible at home for EMS

vitals.time: Follow-up + ACP as an ongoing process

labs.n: PRESENTATION + the proactive outpatient ACP framework
labs.v: 75 y/o man K. Pelletier (CHF NYHA II / EF 38%, CKD stage 3a, HTN, mild cognitive impairment with PRESERVED capacity) at his Medicare Annual Wellness Visit, with NO advance directive, no DPOA-HC, and no POLST — and a stated wish to 'get my paperwork in order.' Proactive outpatient ACP done now, while he can speak for himself, preserves autonomy, reduces surrogate distress, and aligns future care with his values. It is distinct from acute conflict over an EXISTING directive ([[case-092]]), surrogate decision-making for an INCAPACITATED patient with no proxy ([[case-132]]), and capacity assessment for a CURRENT decision/dignity of risk ([[case-116]]). The nurse's flow: confirm capacity → elicit values/goals → educate about the document types and code status in plain language → support surrogate selection + EDUCATION → coordinate document execution per state law → ensure cross-setting PORTABILITY → frame ACP as an ongoing process. Throughout: respect autonomy (including the right to defer/decline) and document objectively.
labs.r: Proactive outpatient ACP for a CAPABLE older adult with serious illness preserves autonomy, reduces surrogate burden, and aligns care with values. Flow: confirm capacity → elicit values → educate (AD types + POLST + code status) → surrogate selection + EDUCATION → execute per state law → portability → ongoing process. Respect autonomy (incl. right to defer/decline); document. RN role: facilitate without coercion.

labs.n: Types of ADVANCE DIRECTIVES + POLST/MOLST distinction + DNR/DNAR clarifications
labs.v: TWO categories of document apply. (A) ADVANCE DIRECTIVES (any capable adult, for FUTURE possible incapacity): the LIVING WILL is an INSTRUCTIONAL directive stating what treatments the patient would WANT or REFUSE in specific future conditions (e.g., mechanical ventilation, artificial nutrition/hydration, dialysis, antibiotics in terminal illness, CPR); the DURABLE POWER OF ATTORNEY FOR HEALTHCARE (DPOA-HC / healthcare proxy / agent) DESIGNATES a SURROGATE to decide if the patient loses capacity — high yield: the DPOA-HC alone is often more useful than a living will because no document anticipates every scenario but an educated surrogate can apply substituted judgment. (B) POLST / MOLST is NOT an advance directive — it is a portable, signed CLINICIAN'S ORDER for CURRENT preferences in seriously ill/frail patients, covering CPR (Attempt vs Do Not Attempt), level of intervention (Full / Selective / Comfort-focused), and medically administered nutrition; it TRAVELS across settings (home, EMS, ED, hospital, SNF), is followed by emergency responders, and SUPPLEMENTS (does NOT replace) the advance directive. CODE STATUS: a DNR/DNAR/AND order limits ONLY CARDIOPULMONARY RESUSCITATION in cardiac arrest — it does NOT mean 'no care' (other treatments continue per plan), it does NOT require a different unit, and it does NOT preclude hospice/aggressive comfort care. Documents are STATE-SPECIFIC (forms, witnesses, notary): execute per state law; review when moving states. Teach in plain language at the patient's health-literacy level.
labs.r: ADVANCE DIRECTIVES: LIVING WILL = treatment preferences for FUTURE conditions; DPOA-HC = NAMES a surrogate (often more useful — no form anticipates everything). POLST/MOLST = portable CLINICIAN'S ORDER for CURRENT preferences in seriously ill/frail pts (CPR/level/nutrition); travels across settings; SUPPLEMENTS — does NOT replace — the AD. DNR/DNAR/AND limits ONLY CPR — not 'no care,' no unit transfer, compatible w/ comfort care. STATE-SPECIFIC documents — execute per state law. RN role: teach the distinctions in plain language.

labs.n: Capacity, surrogate selection + EDUCATION, values, cultural sensitivity
labs.v: CAPACITY (decision-specific, presumed): the four-element test — understand information, appreciate how it applies to him, reason through options, and communicate a stable choice; mild cognitive impairment ALONE, an unwise choice, age, or disagreement do NOT equal incapacity ([[case-116]]). Screen/optimize reversible factors (delirium, depression, pain, sensory aids) before declaring durable incapacity. SURROGATE SELECTION: a DPOA-HC should be someone who knows the patient's values, is WILLING and ABLE to advocate, can manage stress, and is reachable — not necessarily the eldest child or default spouse; he may name an alternate. The patient must EDUCATE the surrogate about his values and likely preferences so the surrogate can apply SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what the patient would want) — not their own preferences — when the time comes. Include the family in the conversation when the patient consents. VALUES elicitation uses OPEN questions ('what makes life meaningful?', 'what worries you most about getting sicker?', 'what trade-offs would you accept?', 'have you watched a loved one go through serious illness?') and avoids leading or scaring. CULTURAL/RELIGIOUS sensitivity: ACP norms vary; some traditions emphasize family-centered or collective decision-making; clarify the patient's preferred role and involve chaplaincy/cultural broker as desired — but always preserve his autonomy. The nurse FACILITATES the conversation, never coerces, and supports the patient's right to DEFER or DECLINE ACP.
labs.r: CAPACITY = decision-specific, presumed: understand/appreciate/reason/communicate stable choice (MCI/age/unwise choice/disagreement ≠ incapacity); optimize reversible factors. SURROGATE = knows values, willing/able to advocate, reachable; EDUCATE the surrogate about values to apply SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT later. Elicit values w/ OPEN questions. Cultural/religious sensitivity (collective decision-making OK if patient chooses); chaplain/broker on request. Facilitate without coercion; respect the right to defer/decline. RN role: facilitate, never coerce.

labs.n: Document execution + cross-setting portability + follow-up + Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: EXECUTION: follow state-specific requirements (witnesses, notary, forms); the LIVING WILL and DPOA-HC are signed by the patient (and witnesses/notary); a POLST is signed by a clinician (and many states also require patient/surrogate signature). PORTABILITY (the most common operational failure point): file in the chart and EHR; give COPIES to the patient, the wife/DPOA-HC, and the alternate (daughter); share with the PCP, cardiology, and the local hospital ED; provide a wallet card; place the POLST in a visible location at home for EMS. Verify documents are actually accessible — a perfect document that lives only in a clinic chart fails when the patient arrives in an ED elsewhere. FOLLOW-UP: ACP is an ongoing PROCESS. Revisit at every AWV and around the 'five Ds' — Decade (new), Death (of someone close), Divorce, new Diagnosis, Decline — and any time goals change; remind him he can change his mind at any time. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT make decisions for him 'because he is older and has multiple conditions'; did NOT treat mild cognitive impairment as automatic incapacity and bypass him; did NOT pressure him toward a particular code status or have him sign documents he did not understand; did NOT tell him a DNR means 'no care' or that he would be moved to a different ward; and did NOT complete documents without ensuring portability and access across settings. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: proactive timing while capacity is intact; capacity confirmed; values-based, plain-language conversation; correct teaching of advance directives vs POLST and accurate DNR clarification; surrogate selected and EDUCATED about values; family included with consent and cultural sensitivity; documents executed per state law and made portable; ongoing-process framing; objective documentation. EXPECTED outcome: his autonomy is preserved, his wishes are clear and accessible, his surrogate is prepared to advocate for him, and the care team can honor his goals across settings.
labs.r: EXECUTION per state law (witnesses/notary); LIVING WILL/DPOA-HC = patient-signed; POLST = clinician-signed. PORTABILITY = the common failure: chart/EHR + copies to patient/wife/agent/daughter + share w/ PCP/cardiology/local hospital + wallet card + POLST visible at home. FOLLOW-UP = ongoing process: every AWV + 'five Ds' (Decade/Death/Divorce/Diagnosis/Decline); he can change his mind. Negatives confirm correct practice (didn't decide for him, didn't treat MCI as incapacity, didn't coerce/pressure, didn't say DNR = no care, didn't skip portability). Protective: proactive timing + capacity + plain-language values conversation + correct AD/POLST teaching + educated surrogate + cultural sensitivity + portable documents + ongoing process + documentation. RN role: facilitate + execute + portability.

labs.v: 48 mL/min/1.73 m^2 (stage 3a CKD, stable; relevant to future ACP decisions about dialysis)
320 pg/mL (mildly elevated, consistent with stable NYHA II HF; relevant to long-term prognosis discussion)
3/5 (mild impairment on screen; formal evaluation confirms decision-making CAPACITY is preserved)

nursesNote: Outpatient clinic — Medicare AWV. 75 y/o man, CHF NYHA II (LVEF 38%), CKD 3a (eGFR 48), HTN, mild cognitive impairment with PRESERVED CAPACITY on prior formal eval. NO advance directive, no DPOA-HC, no POLST on file. He requests proactive ACP. Wife at bedside, adult daughter on speakerphone. ACP flow: (1) CAPACITY confirmed (understands/appreciates/reasons/communicates stable choice; MCI alone ≠ incapacity); reversible factors screened. (2) VALUES elicited with OPEN questions — independence, time with family, quality over length, fears prolonged ICU/mechanical ventilation. (3) EDUCATED in plain language on the two categories: ADVANCE DIRECTIVES — LIVING WILL (future treatment preferences) + DURABLE POA-HC (NAMES a surrogate; often the most useful piece since no form anticipates every scenario); POLST/MOLST — portable CLINICIAN'S ORDER for CURRENT preferences in seriously ill/frail patients (CPR/level/nutrition) that TRAVELS across settings and SUPPLEMENTS (does NOT replace) the AD; DNR/DNAR/AND limits ONLY CPR — NOT 'no care,' no unit transfer, compatible w/ comfort/hospice care. State-specific documents acknowledged. (4) SURROGATE selected — wife as primary DPOA-HC + daughter as alternate — and EDUCATED about his values so they can apply SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT later (not their own preferences). Family included with his consent; cultural/religious context explored; chaplain offered. No coercion; his right to defer/decline respected. (5) EXECUTION per state law (witnesses/notary as required); PCP signed a POLST consistent with his stated wishes (SELECTIVE INTERVENTION + Do Not Attempt CPR). (6) PORTABILITY — copies filed in chart/EHR, given to patient/wife/daughter, shared with cardiology + local hospital + ED; wallet card carried; POLST visible at home for EMS. (7) Framed as an ongoing PROCESS — revisit at next AWV + around the 'five Ds' (Decade/Death/Divorce/Diagnosis/Decline) or any change; he can change his mind anytime. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT decide for him, did NOT treat MCI as automatic incapacity, did NOT pressure a code status or have him sign what he didn't understand, did NOT say DNR = no care, did NOT skip portability. Documentation objective and contemporaneous; team aligned; wife/daughter prepared.
providerOrders: VISIT: Medicare Annual Wellness Visit with Advance Care Planning (ACP) element; outpatient primary care

CAPACITY: confirm decision-making capacity (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate stable choice); screen/optimize reversible factors (delirium, depression, pain, sensory aids); MCI alone does not equal incapacity

ADVANCE CARE PLANNING: facilitate a values-based ACP conversation in plain language; respect autonomy including the right to defer or decline; do not coerce or sign documents the patient does not understand

EDUCATION: teach the distinction between advance directives (living will + durable POA-HC) and POLST/MOLST as a portable clinician's order; clarify what a DNR/DNAR/AND order does and does not mean (only limits CPR; not 'no care'; not unit transfer; compatible with hospice/comfort care)

SURROGATE: support selection of a primary DPOA-HC and an alternate; educate the surrogate(s) about the patient's values so they can apply substituted judgment; involve family with the patient's consent
DOCUMENTS: complete and execute advance directive (living will + DPOA-HC) per state law (witnesses/notary as required); PCP to sign a POLST consistent with the patient's stated preferences

PORTABILITY: file copies in the chart and EHR; provide copies to the patient, primary surrogate (wife), and alternate (daughter); share with cardiology, the local hospital ED, and other specialists; provide a wallet card; place the POLST in a visible location at home for EMS
CULTURAL/SPIRITUAL: explore cultural and religious preferences; offer chaplaincy and cultural broker as desired; preserve the patient's autonomy throughout

FOLLOW-UP: frame ACP as an ongoing process — revisit at each Annual Wellness Visit and around the 'five Ds' (Decade, Death, Divorce, Diagnosis, Decline) or any change in goals; remind the patient he can change his mind at any time

DOCUMENTATION: objective, contemporaneous record of capacity, values elicited, education delivered, surrogate selection and education, documents executed, where copies were sent, and the patient's expressed wishes (in his own words)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters about this proactive ACP visit?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: A capable older adult with serious chronic illness and NO advance directive on file — proactive ACP is indicated while he can speak for himself
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the timing (capable, before a crisis) is what makes ACP most useful. RN role: facilitate proactively.

opts.text: Two categories of document apply: ADVANCE DIRECTIVES (living will + durable POA-HC) for future incapacity, and POLST/MOLST as a portable clinician's order for current preferences in seriously ill/frail patients
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — distinguishing AD from POLST is high-yield. RN role: teach the distinction.

opts.text: Capacity is preserved (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate a stable choice); mild cognitive impairment alone does NOT equal incapacity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — capacity is decision-specific and presumed; MCI/age/unwise choice ≠ incapacity. RN role: confirm capacity.

opts.text: The surrogate (DPOA-HC) must be educated about the patient's values so they can apply substituted judgment later
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a named surrogate is only useful if they actually know the patient's values. RN role: support surrogate education.

opts.text: A DNR/DNAR/AND order limits ONLY CPR — it does NOT mean 'no care,' does NOT require a unit transfer, and is compatible with comfort/hospice care
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a common misunderstanding to correct in teaching. RN role: clarify DNR.

opts.text: Documents must be executed per state law and made PORTABLE across settings (chart/EHR + copies to patient/family + share with PCP/specialists/local hospital + POLST visible at home)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — perfect documents that are not accessible at the point of care fail the patient. RN role: ensure portability.

opts.text: Make the code-status and treatment decisions for him because he is older and has multiple conditions
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Deciding FOR a capable patient ignores his autonomy; the ACP conversation is patient-centered and values-based.

opts.text: Tell him a DNR order means he will receive no care at all and would be moved to a separate ward
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A DNR limits only CPR; other treatments continue per the plan, and no unit transfer is required.

opts.text: Proactive ACP preserves autonomy, aligns future care with the patient's values, and reduces decisional distress for surrogates
opts.rat: CORRECT — the purpose of ACP. Foundation for doing it proactively.

opts.text: A living will states what treatments the patient would want or refuse; a durable POA-HC names a surrogate to decide if the patient cannot — both are advance directives
opts.rat: CORRECT — the AD distinction. Foundation for correct teaching.

opts.text: POLST/MOLST is a portable clinician's ORDER for current preferences in seriously ill/frail patients (CPR, level of intervention, nutrition) and supplements — not replaces — the advance directive
opts.rat: CORRECT — POLST role and distinction from AD. Foundation for cross-setting care.

opts.text: Capacity is decision-specific and presumed; mild cognitive impairment, age, an unwise choice, or disagreement do not by themselves equal incapacity
opts.rat: CORRECT — capacity principles. Foundation for keeping the patient at the center.

opts.text: ACP is an ongoing process; documents should be revisited periodically (e.g., at AWVs and around the 'five Ds') and the patient can change his mind at any time
opts.rat: CORRECT — process/revisit principle. Foundation for sustained alignment with values.

opts.text: Documents are state-specific (forms, witnesses, notary) — execute per state law and review when moving states
opts.rat: CORRECT — legal-execution principle. Foundation for valid documents.

opts.text: A living will alone can anticipate every clinical scenario, so naming a healthcare proxy is unnecessary if a living will exists
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. No form anticipates every scenario; a well-informed DPOA-HC is often the most useful piece of an advance directive.

opts.text: Once an involuntary or admission status changes, the patient automatically loses the right to make any of his own decisions, including ACP
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Decision-making capacity is a clinical determination separate from administrative status, and a capable patient retains decision rights.

opts.text: Confirm decision-making capacity and elicit values/goals using open questions in plain language
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Capacity and values are the foundation of every subsequent step. RN role: confirm capacity + elicit values.

opts.text: Educate about the document types (living will, DPOA-HC, POLST/MOLST) and clarify what a DNR/DNAR order does and does not mean
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Informed choice requires accurate, plain-language understanding. RN role: teach distinctions.

opts.text: Support selection of a primary surrogate and an alternate, and EDUCATE the surrogate(s) about the patient's values
opts.rat: #3 — A named but uninformed surrogate fails substituted judgment. RN role: surrogate selection + education.

opts.text: Complete and execute the documents per state law (and POLST as appropriate) and ensure cross-setting portability
opts.rat: #4 — Execution + portability turn the conversation into care that actually follows him. RN role: execute + make portable.

opts.text: Frame ACP as an ongoing process — schedule reassessment and document objectively
opts.rat: #5 — Sustains alignment with values over time. RN role: revisit + document.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this ACP visit.

opts.text: Confirm capacity, elicit values/goals with open questions, and use plain-language teaching at his health-literacy level
opts.rat: Capacity and values anchor the entire conversation; plain language supports informed choice. RN role: capacity + values + plain language.

opts.text: Explain living will, durable POA-HC, and POLST/MOLST distinctions and clarify what a DNR/DNAR order does and does not mean
opts.rat: Accurate education enables informed decisions. RN role: teach the distinctions.

opts.text: Support selection of a primary and alternate surrogate and educate them about his values so they can apply substituted judgment
opts.rat: An informed surrogate is essential to substituted judgment. RN role: surrogate selection + education.

opts.text: Complete and execute the advance directive per state law; have the PCP sign a POLST consistent with his stated preferences
opts.rat: Valid documents reflecting his wishes are the operational output. RN role: coordinate execution.

opts.text: Ensure cross-setting portability — chart/EHR + copies to patient/family + share with PCP/specialists/local hospital + visible POLST at home
opts.rat: Documents must reach the point of care; portability is the most common operational failure. RN role: ensure access.

opts.text: Respect his autonomy — including the right to defer or decline ACP — and frame ACP as an ongoing process to revisit
opts.rat: Autonomy includes the right to decline; ACP is a process, not a one-time form. RN role: respect + revisit.

opts.text: Explore cultural and religious considerations and offer chaplaincy/cultural broker, while preserving his autonomy
opts.rat: Cultural sensitivity makes ACP authentic to the patient. RN role: include cultural/spiritual context.

opts.text: Decide the code status and treatment plan for him because he is older and has multiple conditions
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — deciding for a capable patient ignores autonomy. ACP is patient-centered.

opts.text: Pressure him to choose DNR and have him sign documents he does not understand
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — coercion and uninformed signing invalidate the process. Educate, do not pressure.

opts.text: Tell him a DNR means no care will be provided and he will be moved to a separate ward
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a DNR limits only CPR; other care continues per plan, with no required unit transfer.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm capacity and elicit values and goals using open questions in plain language
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Anchors the conversation in the patient's own values. RN role: capacity + values.

opts.text: Teach the distinction between living will, durable POA-HC, and POLST/MOLST, and clarify what a DNR/DNAR order does and does not mean
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Informed understanding precedes informed choices. RN role: education.

opts.text: Facilitate surrogate selection and education; include family with his consent and explore cultural/religious context
opts.rat: #3 — Third. A named, informed surrogate is central to substituted judgment. RN role: surrogate selection + education.

opts.text: Complete and execute documents per state law and ensure cross-setting portability and access
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Turns the conversation into operational, accessible care plans. RN role: execute + make portable.

opts.text: Document objectively and contemporaneously and frame ACP as an ongoing process to revisit
opts.rat: #5 — Closing/ongoing. Sustains alignment with values over time. RN role: document + revisit.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action/outcome, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The nurse confirmed capacity, elicited values in plain language, taught the document distinctions accurately, and supported surrogate selection and education
opts.rat: Patient-centered, values-based ACP is the standard. Supports autonomy.

opts.text: The advance directive (living will + DPOA-HC) was executed per state law and the PCP signed a POLST consistent with his stated wishes
opts.rat: Valid documents that match his words. Operationalizes his wishes.

opts.text: Copies were filed in the chart/EHR, given to the patient and surrogates, and shared with cardiology and the local hospital ED — and the POLST is visible at home for EMS
opts.rat: Portability is the key operational success. Documents that follow him.

opts.text: ACP was framed as an ongoing process with planned reassessment at the next AWV and around the 'five Ds' — and he understands he can change his mind anytime
opts.rat: Sustained, values-aligned care over time. Correct framing.

opts.text: Alternative — the team decided his code status without his input because he is older and has multiple conditions
opts.rat: ⚠️ Ignores his preserved capacity and autonomy. Demonstrates the central error to avoid.

opts.text: Alternative — the nurse pressured him to choose DNR and had him sign documents he did not understand
opts.rat: ⚠️ Coercion and uninformed signing invalidate consent. Educate, do not pressure.

opts.text: Alternative — he was told a DNR means 'no care' and that he would be moved to a separate ward
opts.rat: ⚠️ A DNR limits only CPR; other care continues per plan, with no required unit transfer.

opts.text: Alternative — documents were completed but filed only in the clinic chart with no copies shared; in a later ED visit the team did not know his wishes
opts.rat: ⚠️ Portability failure is the most common operational error in ACP. Always share across settings.

opts.text: Alternative — staff treated mild cognitive impairment as automatic incapacity and bypassed him to ask the wife to decide
opts.rat: ⚠️ MCI alone does not equal incapacity; capacity is decision-specific and presumed.

opts.text: He left empowered, with accessible documents, an educated surrogate, and the understanding that he can update his wishes anytime
opts.rat: The ideal outcome of proactive outpatient ACP. Autonomy preserved; wishes accessible.
